rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DOROTHY JEAN BERNSTEIN STUBBLEFIELD LEWIS IRREVOCABLE TRUST DATED 4/13/22
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Petition Pursuantto Probate Code Sections 17200 And 17202 (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] Movants' reliance on Tubbs to argue the Trust is revocable, as the term is used in sections 15800 and 15803, overlooks legally crucial fiduciary differences between the trust instrument in Tubbs and the Lewis Trust in this matter. In Tubbs, the trust provided that the surviving spouse in their capacity as trustee was required to allocate the trust's remaining assets between the revocable surviving spouse's trust and the irrevocable Marital Trust. The Marital Trust and surviving spouse's trust both provided the surviving spouse with a general power of appointment: "At any time during the surviving spouse's life, the trustee shall distribute all or any part of the trust, including accrued income and undistributed income, to such one or more persons and entities, including the surviving spouse or the surviving spouse's estate, and on such terms and conditions, outright, in trust, or by creating further powers of appointment, as the surviving spouse shall request by an acknowledged document that specifically refers to this power of appointment." (Tubbs at 551-52.) The surviving spouse exercised his power of general appointment and appointed all trust assets to himself, effectively divesting the contingent beneficiaries of the Marital Trust. The Tubbs court considered a general power of appointment held by the surviving spouse, who was simultaneously the trustor, that allowed the "powerholder [i.e. the surviving spouse] to act in a nonfiduciary capacity." The court held the powerholder in that instance "could not possibly have breached any fiduciary duties by doing something that was expressly authorized by the [] Trust." (Tubbs at 554). The court specifically pointed out that the beneficiary contesting the general power of appointment "ignore[d] a key feature of a general power of appointment-it enables the donee to act 'in a nonfiduciary capacity' unlike a trustee." (Id. at 555, emphasis in original). Since the trustor in Tubbs was expressly granted a nonfiduciary general power of appointment, in which he had no duty to the beneficiaries of the trust, the court noted that "as a matter of law [trustor] had the same rights as one who could revoke the Trust." (Id. at 556). The court said that the trustor's removal of all trust property "merely implemented a right given to the surviving spouse by the express language of the Trust." (Id.) In the opinion's final paragraph, the Tubbs court noted "[the surviving spouse's] exercise of his power of appointment would have been unobjectionable if he had resigned as trustee before exercising the power. In that scenario, the successor trustee [] would have been required to transfer the assets to [the surviving spouse] once he exercised the power of appointment in his favor. [] [W]e see no reason why the result should be different where [the surviving spouse] was both the donee and the trustee who had no discretion but to follow the terms of the power of appointment." (Id. At 557.) The terms of the Lewis Trust expressly state the opposite of the Tubbs trust regarding the Trustee's fiduciary duty to beneficiaries: "Notwithstanding any other provision of this Trust Agreement, all discretions, powers and authority shall be exercised by the Trustee in a fiduciary capacity, prudently and exclusively in the interest of the beneficiaries." (Trust, Article VII, subsection B, p:10). This provision applies to all beneficiaries, not just Ms. Lewis or Movants. The Lewis Trust, unlike the Tubbs trust, does not expressly contain a non-fiduciary power of appointment, held separate from the trustee's powers. The section which Movants cite to (Article VII, subsection C(1), p:10) is a fiduciary power the Trustee must exercise in the interest of the beneficiaries, unlike the nonfiduciary general power of appointment expressly given to the surviving spouse trustor in Tubbs.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DOROTHY JEAN BERNSTEIN STUBBLEFIELD LEWIS IRREVOCABLE TRUST DATED 4/13/22
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motionto Dismiss Petition Pursuantto Probate Code Sections 17200 And 17202
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] Parties to appear. This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Co-Trustees Patrice Michelle Broussard and Jason Sherrod Williams ("Co-Trustees" or "Movants")'s Motion to Dismiss Petition Pursuant to Probate Code Sections 17200 and 17202 (the "Motion"). Co-Trustees seek an Order dismissing Richard Stephen Broussard's Petition to: (1) Compel Trustees to Disclose Trust Assets and Terms; (2) Remedy Breach of Trust by Trustees; (3) Compel an Accounting; (4) Suspend and Remove Trustees; (5) Appoint Petitioner as Sole Trustee; (6) Order Payment of Attorneys' Fees; (7) Reduce Trustee Compensation; and (8) Prohibit Use of Trust Funds to Defend, filed on August 12, 2024 (the "Petition"), on the grounds that Richard Stephen Broussard lacks standing to petition this Court. Movants' Motion to Dismiss Petition Pursuant to Probate Code Sections 17200 and 17202 is DENIED, as both procedurally and substantively defective. Procedurally Defective Motion Movants filed a document captioned Notice of Motion and Motion to Dismiss Petition Pursuant to Probate Code Sections 17200 and 17202, on January 3, 2025. Movants failed to timely file and serve the required memorandum of points and authorities with their Motion. California Code of Civil Procedure section 1005(b) states, "all moving and supporting papers shall be served and filed at least 16 court days before the hearing." California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1113(a) states, "A party filing a motion, except for a motion listed in rule 3.1114, must serve and file a supporting memorandum. The court may construe the absence of a memorandum as an admission that the motion or special demurrer is not meritorious and cause for its denial." A motion to dismiss is not a motion listed in CRC Rule 3.1114 and therefore requires a supporting memorandum that "contain[s] a statement of facts, a concise statement of the law, evidence and arguments relied on, and a discussion of the statutes, cases, and textbooks cited in support of the position advanced." (CRC, Rule 3.1113(b)). While a memorandum of points and authorities can be filed as a separate document (see CRC, Rule 3.1112(c).), CRC Rule 8.54(a)(2) states that "A motion must be accompanied by a memorandum and, if it is based on matters outside the record, by declarations or other supporting evidence." (Emphasis added.) "[T]he papers filed in support must include the motion itself, a notice on the motion, and a memorandum of points and authorities in support of the motionall three elements must be present for a valid motion." (Luri v. Greenwald (2003) 107 Cal.App.4th 1119, 1126.) "Serving a notice of motion and motionwithout the supporting papers identified therein render[s] the motion untimely." (Weinstein v. Blumberg (2018), 25 Cal.App.5th 316, 321.) Movants filed their supporting memorandum of points and authorities on January 27, 2025, nine court days before the hearing and three days after Petitioner filed his opposition. This is procedurally insufficient.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DOROTHY JEAN BERNSTEIN STUBBLEFIELD LEWIS IRREVOCABLE TRUST DATED 4/13/22
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Petition Pursuantto Probate Code Sections 17200 And 17202 (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] Movants cite no authority supporting the argument that the expressly irrevocable Lewis Trust "is presumed revocable under Probate Code Sections 15800 and 15804 due to the power of appointment and withdrawal of right." (Motion, p:5, paragraph 15, lines 4-8.) Movants cite no authority to support their argument that Article VII, subsection C(1), p:10 of the Trust (i.e. Trustees have the power to "Sell, exchange, lease (for terms extending beyond the termination of the trust or otherwise), rent, mortgage, pledge, assign, transfer or otherwise dispose of all or any part of the trust estate upon such terms and conditions as the Trustee shall determine, with or without notice.") can be read separately from the fiduciary duty language of Article VII, subsection B, p:10. They offer no supporting evidence that Article VII, subsection C(1), p:10 was intended to be a power of appointment under Probate Code section 610. The current income beneficiary of the Trust is still alive. Under the terms of the Lewis Trust, Richard Stephen Broussard is a remainder contingent beneficiary of the instant Trust. Article III of the Lewis Trust provides, "If no descendant of PATRICE MICHELLE BROUSSARD is then living, the Trustee shall divide such assets among the descendants, by Right of Representation, of the Settlor." (Trust, p:2). The descendants of Settlor include: (1) Patrice Michelle Broussard and (2) Richard Stephen Broussard. Therefore, Richard Stephen Broussard has an interest in and is a remainder contingent beneficiary of the expressly irrevocable Lewis Trust and has standing under Probate Code section 24 to bring the Petition under Probate Code section 17200. Dismissal Under Probate Code section 17202 Movants argue the Petition should also, or alternatively, be dismissed pursuant to Probate Code section 17202. Probate Code section 17202 gives a probate court discretion to "dismiss a petition if it appears that the proceeding is not reasonably necessary for the protection of the interests of the trustee or beneficiary." Given the allegations in the verified Petition and in Petitioner's opposition to the Motion that Movants took advantage of Settlor and Movants are substantially mismanaging assets belonging to the Lewis Trust, and supporting documentation provided, the court does not find at this time that the proceeding is not reasonably necessary for the protection of the interests of the beneficiaries. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DOROTHY JEAN BERNSTEIN STUBBLEFIELD LEWIS IRREVOCABLE TRUST DATED 4/13/22
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Petition Pursuantto Probate Code Sections 17200 And 17202 (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Even if Movants had filed the proper documents with their Motion and served them timely, after reviewing and considering the arguments and authorities presented by Movants in both Notice of Motion and Motion to Dismiss Petition Pursuant to Probate Code Sections 17200 and 17202 and the untimely memorandum of points and authorities, the Motion is substantively denied on the grounds that Petitioner Richard Stephen Broussard has standing to seek relief under Probate Code section 17200. Standing as a Contingent Beneficiary of an Irrevocable Trust Probate Code section 17200 provides that "a trustee or beneficiary may petition the court under this chapter concerning the internal affairs of the trust or to determine the existence of the trust." Probate Code section 24(c) defines "beneficiary" as "a person who has any present or future interest, vested or contingent." The California legislature and courts of review have given the probate court jurisdiction "over practically all controversies which might arise between the trustees and those claiming to be beneficiaries under the trust." (Estate of Marie (1941) 18 Cal. 2d 184, 187.) The probate court "permit[s] those whose well-pleaded allegations show that they have an interest in a trust [] to petition the probate court." (Barefoot v. Jennings (2020) 8 Cal. 5th 822, 828.) Movants cite to four cases as a basis for their argument that Richard Stephen Broussard lacks standing to petition as a trust beneficiary: (1) Estate of Giraldin (2012) 55 Cal.4th 1058 ("Giraldin"); (2) Babbitt v. Superior Court (2016) Cal.App.4th 1135 ("Babbitt"); (3) Esslinger v. Cummins (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 517 ("Esslinger"); and (4) Tubbs v. Berkowitz (2020), 47 Cal.App.5th 548 ("Tubbs"). The difference in the rights of beneficiaries to a revocable trust versus an irrevocable trust is explained in the case law Movants cite. In Giraldin, which involved claims by beneficiaries of a revocable trust, the court stated that "once the trust becomes irrevocablethe beneficiaries become the protected persons." (Giraldin at 1070.) When a trust becomes irrevocable, "Those rights which were postponed while the holder of the power to revoke was alive, mature into present and enforceable rights [.]" (Ibid.) In Babbitt, which also involved claims by beneficiaries of a revocable trust, the court stated, "after a settlor dies and the trust or a portion of the trust becomes irrevocable, section 17200 gives a contingent beneficiary standing to petition the probate court" (Babbitt at 1144.) In Esslinger, while the court held that a trustee does not have a statutory duty to account to remainder beneficiaries of a revocable trust, "a remainder beneficiary may make a written request to the trustee for information [under Probate Code section 16061]. If the trustee does not comply with a reasonable request within 60 days after the request, and no report or account had been made within the six months preceding the remainder beneficiary's request, then a remainder beneficiary may petition the court under Probate Code section 17200, subdivision (b)(7), to compel the trustee to report information about the trust or to account." (Esslinger at 542, emphasis in original.) The court reads the instant Trust as a whole. "To interpret a trust instrument, the instrument itself is paramount.[Citation.] We construe all parts of the instrument in relation to one another to form a consistent whole. (Prob. C. sec. 21121.) This rule manifests our respect for the intelligence and effort of the drafters, whose intent deserves our allegiance." (Autonomous Region of Narcotics Anonymous v. Narcotics Anonymous World Servs., Inc. (2022) 77 Cal. App. 5th 950, 960-61.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DOROTHY JEAN BERNSTEIN STUBBLEFIELD LEWIS IRREVOCABLE TRUST DATED 4/13/22
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Petition Pursuantto Probate Code Sections 17200 And 17202 (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] When The Dorothy Jean Bernstein Stubblefield Lewis Irrevocable Trust, dated April 13, 2022, (the "Lewis Trust") was created, it was created as an irrevocable trust. The Lewis Trust, on page one, states "This Trust Agreement and each trust estate created in this Trust Agreement is expressly declared to be irrevocable, and the Settlor expressly waives all right and power, acting alone or with others, to alter, amend or change the terms and conditions of this Trust Agreement in whole or in part." (Trust, p:1.) Dorothy Jean Bernstein Stubblefield Lewis, in her capacity as the settlor, never had the power to revoke the Lewis Trust, and the Lewis Trust property was never "considered property of the settlor for the settlor's lifetime" as it would be in a revocable trust. (See, Estate of Giraldin (2012) 55 Cal.4th 1058, 1065-1066.) Despite the plain language of the Lewis Trust that it is irrevocable, Movants argue the Trust is de facto a revocable trust, and the standing issue is governed by authority concerning revocable trusts. Movants cite to Tubbs in conjunction with Probate Code sections 15800 and 15803 as the basis for their argument that "[a] person holding a general power of appointment or the right to withdraw property from the trust holds the rights of one with the power to revoke a trust." (Motion, par 15, 5:17-19.) Probate Code section 15800 expressly applies "during the time that a trust is revocable and at least one person holding the power to revoke the trust, in whole or in part, is competent." Section 15800 has two requirements: (1) that the Trust be revocable and (2) that at least one person holds the power to revoke the trust. Probate Code section 15803 states that "The holder of a presently exercisable general power of appointment or power to withdraw property from the trust has the rights of a person holding the power to revoke the trusts that are provided by Sections 15800 to 15802, inclusive, to the extent of the holder's power over the trust property." Movants cite to Article VII, subsection C(1), p:10 of the Trust, to argue the Trust is revocable. Article VII, subsection C(1) states Trustees have the power to "Sell, exchange, lease (for terms extending beyond the termination of the trust or otherwise), rent, mortgage, pledge, assign, transfer or otherwise dispose of all or any part of the trust estate upon such terms and conditions as the Trustee shall determine, with or without notice." Movants argue this language gives them a power of appointment. Probate Code section 610(f) provides a "'Power of appointment' means a power that enables a powerholder acting in a nonfiduciary capacity to designate a recipient of an ownership interest in or another power of appointment over the appointive property." (Emphasis added.)
